message:
storage: policy_denied_audit table + write path (ft-h90rh)

Follow-up to ft-6mmyp's tracing-only MVP: adds a persistent,
SQL-queryable record for policy-denied MCP mutations so operators can
reconstruct deny attempts outside the log stream.

New in storage.rs:

- SCHEMA_VERSION bumped 23 → 24.
- Migration v24 creates `policy_denied_audit` (ts_ms, agent_id,
  tool_name, intent_hash, reason, reason_code, rule_id, decision) plus
  idx_policy_denied_audit_ts and idx_policy_denied_audit_tool_ts.
  Idempotent via CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS; down_sql rolls it back.
- PolicyDeniedAuditRecord struct with stable reason_code/decision
  constants (REASON_CODE_DENIED, REASON_CODE_REQUIRE_APPROVAL, ...).
- WriteCommand::RecordPolicyDenialAudit variant with a matching label
  arm on Self::label().
- record_policy_denial_audit_sync + writer dispatch: INSERT ... VALUES
  bound parameters, same pattern as record_audit_action_sync.
- StorageHandle::record_policy_denial_audit (+ cx-first
  record_policy_denial_audit_with_cx) routing through the writer
  thread. No re-redaction: `reason` is expected to arrive
  policy-engine-redacted from the caller.

Deliberately out of scope in this shipment (per active concurrent edit
on policy.rs by another pane, left untouched to avoid collision): the
policy.rs convenience wrapper and the wire-up from
mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation in mcp_tools.rs. Those need a separate slot
with the right reservations; filing ft-<new> as the wiring bead.

No tests added in this shipment: the existing SCHEMA_VERSION assertions
at storage.rs:18835+ use the constant symbolically, so the bump is
transparent; migration framework is idempotent and the table-creation
SQL is textbook. A dedicated round-trip test (insert + select) belongs
with the wiring bead that exercises the full path.

Agent: RusticMeadow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-4qom2","title":"[SEV] tmux cmd_queue cap only enforced at pop site; unbounded growth when state != Idle","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs TmuxDomainState.cmd_queue has CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000 enforced only inside send_next_command at line 289 via cmd_queue.drain(..excess). send_next_command early-returns at line 286-288 when state != Idle, so the drain-oldest logic never runs if tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse.

Four push sites append to cmd_queue with NO cap check:
- line 196: Event::LayoutChange -> ListAllPanes
- line 223: Event::SessionChanged -> ListCommands
- line 234: Event::WindowAdd -> ListAllWindows
- line 375: split_pane (user split) -> SplitPane

Concrete interleaving: (1) tmux sends a command, enters State::WaitingForResponse; (2) tmux server is slow/hung (real-world scenario: remote tmux on lagging SSH link, tmux server overloaded, Event::Guarded response delayed); (3) GUI user resizes window repeatedly -> stream of Event::LayoutChange -> each push_back at line 196 runs without cap check; (4) send_next_command early-returns because state != Idle. Queue grows unbounded -> memory leak, eventual OOM.

The CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH comment at line 32-33 explicitly advertises 'prevent unbounded memory growth during event storms' as a safety invariant. That invariant is currently only upheld at the pop site, where it can't run under the exact condition (stuck tmux) that event storms exploit.

Fix: extract the cap-enforcement logic into a push_command_capped helper that applies the drain-oldest + warn/error logging at every push site. The helper runs under the cmd_queue lock (just acquired by the caller), so it's atomic with the push and has no additional locking cost.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:17:26.601099Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.160715Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.158111Z","close_reason":"Completed: added push_command_capped() helper and routed all 4 production push sites (LayoutChange/SessionChanged/WindowAdd/split_pane) through it. The hard cap is now enforced at push time, not only at pop time — closing the unbounded-growth window when tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse. cargo check passes clean. Commit 1055cffc.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4r2ib","title":"[mission][dispatch] MissionDispatcher reports accepted dispatch without calling WorkflowRunner::handle_detection","description":"In `crates/frankenterm-core/src/mission_dispatch.rs:200-226`, `dispatch_single()` builds a synthetic detection, then only calls `runner.find_matching_workflow(&detection)` and fabricates `accepted: true` plus a synthetic `execution_id` when a workflow exists. It never invokes `WorkflowRunner::handle_detection()`, so no lock acquisition, no concurrency-limit enforcement, no storage row, and no real workflow start happen.

Impact: callers receive a success-shaped `DispatchResult` that claims work was accepted even though the runner was never asked to start anything.

Fix direction: bridge the sync mission loop to the async runner start path with a bounded runtime, map the actual `WorkflowStartResult` into `DispatchResult`, and only emit started metadata from a real start result.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T20:09:03.405716Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T20:16:34.545322Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T20:16:34.544924Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 0761bb33: mission dispatch now calls WorkflowRunner::handle_detection and returns real start metadata","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4z7vh","title":"RobotCommands::Workflow builds PolicyEngine without require_prompt_active/command_gate/rules — bypasses operator safety config","description":"AUTH BYPASS (robot mode mutations, Severity: Medium).

crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs has 10 PolicyEngine::new call sites. 8 of them wire require_prompt_active from config + .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()). Two do NOT, both under RobotCommands::Workflow:

Site 1 (main.rs:19831, RobotCommands::Workflow::Run):

  let policy_engine = PolicyEngine::new(
      config.safety.rate_limit_per_pane,
      config.safety.rate_limit_global,
      false, // Don't require prompt active for robot mode
  )
  .with_tuning(&config.tuning);

No command_gate, no policy_rules, and require_prompt_active hardcoded false. This engine is fed into CxPolicyInjector → WorkflowRunner; any workflow step that calls send_text (e.g., handle_compaction sending '/compact', handle_usage_limit sending '/refresh') bypasses:
  - the operator's safety.require_prompt_active=true policy,
  - every [[safety.rules]] custom gate,
  - the [safety.command_gate] allow/deny list.

Site 2 (main.rs:20511, RobotCommands::Workflow::Abort): identical construction. abort_execution_with_cx runs compensation steps through the same injector; any send_text in a compensation path bypasses the same three surfaces.

Compare the correctly-gated paths:
  - RobotCommands::Send at main.rs:17652 uses config.safety.require_prompt_active AND .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules.
  - Non-robot workflow invocations at main.rs:21361, 21533, 25051, 27347 all wire the full policy surface.

NOT a duplicate of ft-48ah6 (read-only policy): this bead is specifically about the write/mutation surface (workflow's send_text / command-bearing steps). ft-48ah6 covered the read/search side.

Fix: replace the hardcoded 'false' with config.safety.require_prompt_active at both sites, and chain .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()) before passing the engine to PolicyGatedInjector. Rate-limit args are already correct.

Regression test: a send inside a robot-invoked workflow must be DENIED when config.safety.require_prompt_active=true AND no prompt is active, matching the direct-send gate behavior. Currently it is allowed.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane5","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:46:59.925741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084786Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084480Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 834b59f2. Both RobotCommands::Workflow::Run (main.rs:19831) and ::Abort (main.rs:20520) now build PolicyEngine with config.safety.require_prompt_active and chain .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules — matching the construction at RobotCommands::Send (main.rs:17652) and non-robot workflow sites (21361/21533/25051/27347). Regression test robot_workflow_policy_engine_denies_send_when_prompt_required_ft_4z7vh asserts SendText is denied when require_prompt_active=true + command_running. Test binary compile is currently blocked by pre-existing concurrent-agent breakage (E0603 macro select private, E0063 missing defer_fts_triggers field) unrelated to this fix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-h8da2","title":"[security][P3] Verify Redactor is applied on every pane-content read path (design-audit)","description":"## Context

Pane content is stored UNREDACTED at write time: crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:12363 append_segment_sync inserts the raw content string directly into output_segments.content with no Redactor invocation, while peer write paths (set_event_note_sync at :12603 redacts; record_audit_action_redacted at :6647 redacts; undo metadata at :6701 redacts) DO call Redactor::new().redact(...).

This is a deliberate design choice: preserve full fidelity at rest, apply redaction at the serving boundary (get-text, search, event emission, export, audit, web/** handlers, MCP tool envelopes). The README calls this out: 'Captured output is redacted before being returned through any API surface.'

The design is sound but fragile: every read path must redact. A missing redaction on even ONE read path leaks secrets (API keys, session tokens, agent-prompt credentials) that an operator reasonably assumed were filtered.

## Scope of this task

NOT a code change. A deliberate audit of every pane-content read path in the repo, producing a short matrix:

| Read path | File:line | Redacts? |
|-----------|-----------|----------|

Paths to check (non-exhaustive):
- wa.get_text MCP tool (mcp_tools.rs)
- wa.search MCP tool (mcp_tools.rs)
- ft robot get-text CLI (main.rs)
- ft robot search CLI (main.rs)
- /panes /events /search /stream/* web handlers (web/handlers.rs, web/sse.rs)
- ft export (export.rs)
- ft audit / ft history (storage access paths)
- ft replay (replay.rs decoded frame payloads — FrameType::Output content flows back out verbatim)
- Distributed aggregator forwarding (distributed.rs)

Each row of the matrix should cite the specific line that does or does NOT invoke Redactor::redact (or equivalent).

## Why P3

Not a known vulnerability — no specific read path is known to skip redaction. But the fragile invariant + large surface means a quiet leak is the most plausible way secrets end up exposed. Cheap to verify; one-time cost.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Matrix committed under docs/security/ with every pane-content read path enumerated.
- Any path missing redaction becomes its own follow-up bead (per-path P1 at that point).
- If no gaps found, matrix serves as a regression benchmark future refactors can diff against.

-{"id":"ft-h90rh","title":"[security][follow-up ft-6mmyp] Persist policy-denied MCP mutations to audit_actions table (not just tracing log)","description":"## Context

ft-6mmyp primary fix shipped: mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation now emits structured tracing::warn! on every deny / require_approval under target ft::security::policy with tool / command / decision / rule_id / reason fields. That closes the 'zero observability' gap for denied MCP mutations.

The bead's ideal (fuller) fix was a record_audit_action_redacted write so denied attempts appear in the same audit_actions table as successful ones — enables SQL-queryable forensics and parity with the wa.events_annotate post-mutation audit at mcp_tools.rs:4330-4352.

## Why deferred

record_audit_action_redacted is async. The current sync gate helper at mcp_tools.rs:177-231 can't await. The callers (tx_run/rollback, mission_pause/resume/abort) all do already live inside a runtime.block_on scope, but the helper is invoked BEFORE that block_on — specifically to fail fast without opening a StorageHandle. Opening storage inside the helper on the deny path would:
  - Open a new StorageHandle (~100ms cold path)
  - Require passing db_path (currently the helper only takes &Config)
  - Add a runtime.block_on inside a sync fn that may already be inside a tokio runtime → panic risk

## Fix path

Either:
(a) Push the audit write into the deny branch of each of the 5 caller handlers (they already have runtime.block_on scopes) — duplicates ~10 lines per caller but avoids helper signature changes.
(b) Refactor mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation to return a deny-decision value and have the caller write the audit row plus the error envelope — single point of responsibility per tool.
(c) Change the helper signature to async fn mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation(config, db_path, ...) -> ... and have callers await it inside their existing runtime.block_on.

(b) is cleanest. Roughly:

    enum GateOutcome {
        Allow,
        Deny { envelope: McpResult<Vec<Content>>, audit: AuditActionRecord },
        RequireApproval { envelope: McpResult<Vec<Content>>, audit: AuditActionRecord },
    }
    fn mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation(config, summary, command_text, start) -> GateOutcome

Callers match, write the audit via their existing storage in the deny/approval branches, then return the envelope.

## Acceptance Criteria

- After deny/approval, audit_actions has a row with result='denied' or 'require_approval', decision_reason=policy_reason, action_kind matching the tool.
- Integration test per tool: seed a deny-config, call tool, assert audit row shape.
- Tracing log emission (shipped under ft-6mmyp main fix) remains in place as a second signal.

(a) Push the audit write into the deny branch of each of the 5 caller handlers (they already have runtime.block_on scopes) — duplicates ~10 lines per caller but avoids helper signature changes.
(b) Refactor mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation to return a deny-decision value and have the caller write the audit row plus the error envelope — single point of responsibility per tool.
(c) Change the helper signature to async fn mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation(config, db_path, ...) -> ... and have callers await it inside their existing runtime.block_on.

(b) is cleanest. Roughly:

    enum GateOutcome {
        Allow,
        Deny { envelope: McpResult<Vec<Content>>, audit: AuditActionRecord },
        RequireApproval { envelope: McpResult<Vec<Content>>, audit: AuditActionRecord },
    }
    fn mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation(config, summary, command_text, start) -> GateOutcome

Callers match, write the audit via their existing storage in the deny/approval branches, then return the envelope.

## Acceptance Criteria

- After deny/approval, audit_actions has a row with result='denied' or 'require_approval', decision_reason=policy_reason, action_kind matching the tool.
- Integration test per tool: seed a deny-config, call tool, assert audit row shape.
- Tracing log emission (shipped under ft-6mmyp main fix) remains in place as a second signal.

 {"id":"ft-lekgj","title":"[MEDIUM] HeadlessMuxServer::Heartbeat silently ACKs unknown peers, producing split-brain with pruned federation state","description":"# Defect

## File + line

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/headless_mux_server.rs:423` — `HeadlessMuxServer::handle_request`, `RemoteRequest::Heartbeat` arm.

## Concrete failure path

```rust
RemoteRequest::Heartbeat { from, pane_count } => {
    if let Some(peer) = self.peers.get_mut(&from.node_id) {
        peer.last_heartbeat_at = epoch_ms();
        peer.pane_count = pane_count;
        peer.status = PeerStatus::Connected;
    }
    RemoteResponse::HeartbeatAck
}
```

**If the heartbeat arrives from a node that is NOT in `self.peers`, the handler silently drops it and still returns `HeartbeatAck`.** This is reachable in two natural situations:

1. **Post-prune race.** Peer N missed enough heartbeats to be marked `Unreachable` by `check_peer_health()`, then removed by `prune_unreachable_peers()`. The peer is still running, its network partition heals, and it sends its next heartbeat. Server has no record of N, silently ACKs, peer thinks federation is healthy, server thinks it has fewer peers. Classic split-brain.
2. **Heartbeat before JoinFederation.** A misconfigured or restarted peer starts heartbeating before its `JoinFederation` roundtrip completes (race on a lossy network where JoinFederation gets reordered behind the first Heartbeat). Server silently ACKs. Peer sees success. Server keeps acting without the peer in its registry.

In both cases:
- `ListPeers` on the server returns a stale list (missing the real peer).
- `federated_pane_count` undercounts — pane_count from the unknown peer is thrown away.
- No telemetry, no warn log.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer (split-brain between peer's belief and server's registry), affecting federation telemetry and potentially workload scheduling if `federated_pane_count` feeds a placement decision.
- Not a crash, not data corruption.
- Recovery requires the peer to notice it's not in ListPeers and re-`JoinFederation` — the peer has no prompt to do that today because HeartbeatAck looks successful.

## Fix sketch

Return a specific error when the peer is unknown so the sender can re-JoinFederation and recover automatically:

```rust
RemoteRequest::Heartbeat { from, pane_count } => {
    if let Some(peer) = self.peers.get_mut(&from.node_id) {
        peer.last_heartbeat_at = epoch_ms();
        peer.pane_count = pane_count;
        peer.status = PeerStatus::Connected;
        RemoteResponse::HeartbeatAck
    } else {
        RemoteResponse::Error {
            code: "peer_not_federated".into(),
            message: format!("node {} has not joined this server; re-send JoinFederation", from.node_id),
        }
    }
}
```

Clients receiving `peer_not_federated` should re-send `JoinFederation`. The error code is stable so clients can pattern-match.

## Tests

* `ft_lekgj_heartbeat_for_unknown_peer_returns_error` — Server never saw `JoinFederation`, Heartbeat returns `Error { code: peer_not_federated, .. }` instead of `HeartbeatAck`.
* `ft_lekgj_heartbeat_after_prune_returns_error` — Peer joined, went unreachable, got pruned, then sent a fresh heartbeat; same error surface so the peer knows to rejoin.
* `ft_lekgj_heartbeat_for_known_peer_still_acks` — Regression: the happy path still yields `HeartbeatAck` and updates last_heartbeat_at/pane_count/status.

## Scope

Single file (`headless_mux_server.rs`). No public-API break — `RemoteResponse::Error` already exists and is already used for other failure modes in the same match.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:38:51.321265Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:27.477333Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:42:27.477025Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-lokam","title":"[LOW] diag bundle environment.json leaks absolute workspace cwd; tracing fields leak operator paths","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #6 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
Three logging/telemetry surfaces all come out clean on the obvious
secret-leak vectors (no bearer-token / API-key / pane-content
fields in tracing calls; no /etc/ or .ssh/ reads in diag
collection). One defense-in-depth gap remains: path privacy.

## Findings

### F1 [LOW] diag bundle environment.json includes absolute cwd
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/diagnostic.rs:87-89
  ```rust
  cwd: std::env::current_dir()
      .ok()
      .map(|p| p.display().to_string()),
  ```
- Diag bundles (`ft diag bundle`) are often attached to bug reports
  and shared outside the host. An absolute cwd reveals:
  - the user's home path segment (`/Users/<username>/...` or
    `/home/<username>/...`),
  - the project path (`/Users/alice/projects/secret-startup-pitch-deck/`),
  - on some setups, mount points that hint at filesystem layout.
- Severity: LOW. Users implicitly share the workspace when
  debugging, but the current behavior removes the choice.

### F2 [LOW] tracing fields log operator-private paths
- Multiple `tracing::[info|warn|debug]!(..., path = %X.display(), ...)`
  call sites scatter socket paths, DB paths, and workspace paths
  through structured logs. Representative hits (non-exhaustive):
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/native_events.rs:258, 302, 331,
      362, 401 — unix socket paths.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs:1118 —
      `info_span!("telemetry_store_open", path = %db_path.display())`.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs:1375, 2658, 2814 —
      socket paths on accept failures.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/build_coord.rs:265 —
      `meta_path = %self.meta_path.display()`.
- Severity: LOW. These logs land in the user's local log file,
  not network-visible by default, but they do end up in diag
  bundles when `collect_recent_log_lines` is called.

## Non-findings verified in the same pass
- Bearer tokens in structured logs: grep across
  `tracing::{info,debug,warn,error}!` call sites for
  `token|bearer|secret|password|api_key` returned zero production
  hits. Clean.
- Pane content in tracing fields: no production code logs
  `segment.content`, `delta_text`, or `pane_text`. Only test
  `assert_eq!(segment.content, ...)` references found. Clean.
- Diag bundle reading privileged files: no `/etc/`, `.ssh/`,
  `.aws/`, `.gnupg/` reads in `diagnostic.rs`. The bundle only
  reads the ft SQLite DB (already redacted via `redact_events`)
  and writes to the workspace-local `diag_dir`. Clean.
- Environment variables in diag bundle: `gather_environment`
  captures only OS / arch / rust_version / wa_version / cwd —
  no `std::env::vars()` dump. Clean.
- Summarize_config: the docstring already calls out "no paths or
  secrets" and the implementation confirms — only numeric/boolean
  config values are copied over. Clean.

## Fix direction
- F1: replace absolute cwd with just the basename, or gate
  inclusion behind `DiagnosticOptions.include_cwd: bool` default
  `false`. Concretely at diagnostic.rs:87:
  ```rust
  cwd: if opts.include_cwd {
      std::env::current_dir().ok().map(|p| p.display().to_string())
  } else {
      std::env::current_dir()
          .ok()
          .and_then(|p| p.file_name().map(|n| n.to_string_lossy().into_owned()))
  },
  ```
- F2: when a log line is included in a diag bundle via
  `collect_recent_log_lines` (or equivalent), run it through the
  existing `Redactor` path-aware rules. Non-bundle in-process logs
  stay as-is since they aid operator debugging on the local box.

## Severity rationale
- LOW (P2): neither finding reveals a secret, and both sit behind
  an operator action (`ft diag bundle` + sharing it). The fix
  shrinks the unintentional-share footprint but doesn't close a
  live exploit.

## Related
- ft-ybtyg (598a7c13) — snapshot/checkpoint hashing honesty.
- ft-2guux (doc reality-check) — complementary; this bead is the
  diagnostics-exfil counterpart.

 {"id":"ft-ltpog","title":"native_events: decode errors logged at debug, invisible in production","description":"# [MEDIUM] native_events: malformed wire events logged at debug

**Subsystem:** native_events / protocol_recovery
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/native_events.rs (`handle_connection_with_cx`)

## The hole

When wire-event decoding fails (invalid JSON, bad base64, unknown event
type), the handler emitted:

\`\`\`rust
Err(err) => {
    debug!(error = %err, "failed to decode native event (cx path)");
}
\`\`\`

A malformed wire event is a protocol anomaly — version skew between
frankenterm and the mux-server, on-wire corruption, or a hostile peer
writing into the native-events socket. Operators routinely filter
`debug!` in production, so the signal was effectively lost.

Contrast with the `MAX_EVENT_LINE_BYTES` path a few lines above, which
already emits at `warn!`. Same class of anomaly, inconsistent level.

## Fix

Promoted the decode-error log to `warn!`. The `warn!` import was already
present — no dependency change.

Future work (separate bead): attach a telemetry counter for
decode-failure rate so it can drive a SLI rather than relying solely on
log sampling.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:39.950825Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:46:57.568904Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:46:57.568595Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["native-events","observability"]}

 {"id":"ft-ybtyg","title":"[MEDIUM] session checkpoint + snapshot files have no integrity check; 'BLAKE3' docs vs SipHash reality","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #5 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
Two related gaps in the session-persistence trust story:

1. Snapshot files persisted to SQLite (`session_checkpoints`,
   `mux_pane_state`) have NO integrity check on load. An attacker
   with DB write access — or a malicious snapshot bundle imported
   via diag tooling — can forge arbitrary checkpoint rows, and the
   restore path will replay them. The restored-command injection
   vector closed by ft-kegvt (commit 969b9e02) is one consequence;
   the root cause is that nothing cryptographically binds a
   checkpoint to a trusted writer.

2. Documentation drift amplifies the problem: snapshot_engine.rs:14
   advertises "BLAKE3 hash → dedup (skip if unchanged)" while the
   actual helper `compute_state_hash` at
   snapshot_engine.rs:1641-1651 uses
   `std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher` (SipHash-24, not a
   cryptographic hash). Even if the dedup-only role makes SipHash
   adequate for THAT purpose, the README/inline doc claim misleads
   future auditors into thinking integrity is enforced.

## Evidence

### Missing integrity verification
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:466-474 and
  :509-517: `INSERT INTO session_checkpoints (..., state_hash,
  ...)` with the literal string `'restore'` as the state_hash
  column value. No hash is computed, stored, or later verified.
- storage.rs:609 schema comment claims `state_hash TEXT NOT NULL,
  -- BLAKE3 of serialized state for dedup`, but the INSERT sites
  (:2031, :2268, :2589) either pass a computed SipHash hex string
  (from `compute_state_hash`) or the hardcoded `'restore'`.
- grep confirms zero `verify_snapshot`, `snapshot_hash`,
  `hmac`, `HMAC`, or `verify.*checkpoint` sites across
  `snapshot_engine.rs` + `session_restore.rs`. Load paths read
  `state_hash` for dedup-skip ONLY, never for integrity check.

### Documentation drift
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/snapshot_engine.rs:14 — the module
  docstring says:
      `├── BLAKE3 hash  → dedup (skip if unchanged)`
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/snapshot_engine.rs:1641-1651:
      ```rust
      fn compute_state_hash(panes: &[PaneInfo]) -> String {
          use std::hash::{Hash, Hasher};
          let mut hasher =
              std::collections::hash_map::DefaultHasher::new();
          ...
          ids.hash(&mut hasher);
          ...
          format!("{:016x}", hasher.finish())
      }
      ```
  That's SipHash-24 formatted as a 16-hex-char u64. Not BLAKE3.
  No `blake3::` / `sha2::` imports in snapshot_engine.rs.

## Impact / trust boundary
Integrity-less checkpoint storage means that any path which ends
up writing to the SQLite DB or shipping a DB file between hosts
(session bundle export/import, diag bundle with session data,
backup→restore, snapshot migration between workspaces) has no way
to detect tampering.

The ft-kegvt fix sanitizes CR/LF/ESC/C0 at the `launch_agent`
sanitizer boundary, which blunts the most dangerous class of
tamper-payload. But it doesn't stop an attacker from, e.g.,
forging checkpoint rows that silently restore into the wrong
workspace, swap window/tab ids to masquerade as a different
session, or ship tampered pane metadata for future replays.

Severity: MEDIUM. Needs DB write access or bundle-import trust
crossing — same class as ft-kegvt. Worth closing proactively
before snapshot import is exposed over any wider surface.

## Fix direction

1. Replace the dedup hash with a real BLAKE3 implementation
   (there's already a `blake3` dependency pulled in via
   `storage.rs` + `codec`). Keep the dedup role but also use the
   hash as the integrity witness on restore.
2. Drop the hardcoded `'restore'` placeholder at session_restore.rs:
   467, 510 — either compute a real state_hash over the
   PaneIdMap or make the column `TEXT NULL` and document why the
   restore-time insert has no pane state to hash yet.
3. On load, recompute the hash over the deserialized snapshot and
   compare to the stored value; fail loudly on mismatch with an
   actionable error ("snapshot integrity check failed: stored
   hash X, recomputed Y — refusing to restore").
4. For cross-host snapshot bundles (future), add an HMAC keyed
   from a workspace-local secret in `.ft/hmac.key` so bundles
   cannot be silently swapped between workspaces.
5. Fix the docstring drift at snapshot_engine.rs:14 in lockstep
   with whichever real hash gets adopted.

## Non-findings verified in the same pass
- MCP bearer token validation: no bearer surface in stdio MCP
  (mcp_bridge.rs). Auth is out-of-band via OS process context.
  Clean.
- `wa.send` approval enforcement in non-TTY: mcp_tools.rs:1930-1955
  properly handles `InjectionResult::RequiresApproval` — injection
  gated on `.is_allowed()` at line 1955. Approval-required actions
  return the envelope back, do not auto-bypass. Clean.
- Distributed mode token validation: `constant_time_eq` at
  distributed.rs:209-222 is correctly implemented. `validate_token`
  at :248-289 uses it for both identity and secret. Clean.
- Agent-mail contact handshake: out of scope per AGENTS.md rule
  (NEVER touch agent-mail service). Service-enforced; client
  bridge has no local contact-approval logic to audit. Out of
  scope by policy.

## Related
- ft-kegvt (969b9e02) — closed the CR/LF injection vector that
  could ride a tampered `command` field through the restore path.
  This bead is the structural fix; ft-kegvt was the sanitizer at
  the exit boundary.
- ft-security-mcp-path-traversal (30988e53) — arbitrary-file R/W
  via MCP; same trust-boundary-crossing theme.
- ft-xv561 (070117a0) — ReDoS via user-pack regexes.

 {"id":"ft-ych74","title":"[review][LOW] release.yml smoke test excludes windows-latest — ft-windows-amd64.exe ships without --version/--help/doctor probe","description":".github/workflows/release.yml:68-80 gates the Smoke test binary step with:

    if: matrix.target == 'x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu' || startsWith(matrix.target, 'aarch64-apple') || startsWith(matrix.target, 'x86_64-apple')

The windows-latest / x86_64-pc-windows-msvc target (matrix entry at :48-51) is deliberately skipped. That matters because the smoke step is the only place the workflow exercises:

    "$BINARY" --version
    "$BINARY" version --full
    "$BINARY" --help
    "$BINARY" doctor --json

Any Windows-specific regression (path separator bug, ANSI/console handling, missing crate feature for msvc, clap rendering) ships to users because the release step (:151 action-gh-release) uploads ft-windows-amd64.exe without ever invoking it.

aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu is also skipped (the condition only matches x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu for linux), so the linux/arm64 binary is equally unexercised — two gaps, not one.

Fix: drop the if: guard entirely (smoke test runs on every matrix row) OR extend the condition to include aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu and x86_64-pc-windows-msvc, adjusting the shell to pwsh on windows (Compress-Archive uses pwsh already at :93 so the pattern exists).

Severity: LOW — pre-launch for v0.1.0 where no Windows users exist yet, but absent a smoke check the first Windows release can ship broken and only surface via install.sh failures in the field.

-{"id":"ft-yj375","title":"[security][P1] wa.state MCP tool + ft robot state CLI return pane title/cwd UNREDACTED (mismatched invariant with /panes)","description":"## Finding

Audit under ft-h8da2 (docs/security/read-path-redaction-matrix.md) confirms: wa.state MCP tool and ft robot state CLI serve pane `title` and `cwd` **unredacted**, while the sibling web handler on `GET /panes` **does** redact them.

### Evidence

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_types.rs:817-834 — McpPaneState::from_pane_info:

      title: info.title.clone(),
      cwd: info.cwd.clone(),

- crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:5501-5518 — PaneState::from_pane_info (CLI): same pattern, same unredacted clone.

- crates/frankenterm-core/src/web/handlers.rs:64-70 — PaneView::from_record (web): **CORRECT** reference implementation:

      title: r.title.map(|t| redactor.redact(&t)),
      cwd: r.cwd.map(|c| redactor.redact(&c)),

### Threat

Pane titles are set by whatever process the pane hosts. Agents that bake credentials into the title (e.g. `codex --api-key sk-ant-...` visible in `argv[0]` → terminal title) or workflows that `printf '\\e]0;token=\\\\a'` will leak those values through wa.state and ft robot state.

A peer MCP agent that calls wa.state as part of a normal swarm-wide health check receives the raw title; a network-exposed ft robot state call over a subprocess bridge does the same. Redaction was applied to the web /panes endpoint for this exact reason; the MCP and CLI peers must match.

### Fix

Two equivalent options:

Option A (one-line per call-site, web-matching): in the wa.state and ft robot state handlers, after `McpPaneState::from_pane_info` / `PaneState::from_pane_info`, run the fields through a static `Redactor`:

    let redactor = Redactor::new();
    for s in &mut states {
        if let Some(t) = s.title.as_mut() { *t = redactor.redact(t); }
        if let Some(c) = s.cwd.as_mut()   { *c = redactor.redact(c); }
    }

Option B: change the from_pane_info signatures to accept `&Redactor` and redact inside (symmetrical with web/handlers.rs:64-70).

Option A is smaller-diff and doesn't ripple through test fixtures; preferred.

### Acceptance Criteria

- wa.state envelope serializes title/cwd through Redactor::redact.
- ft robot state envelope serializes title/cwd through Redactor::redact.
- Regression test: seed a pane with title containing `sk-ant-api03-XXXX`, invoke both surfaces, assert the raw token does not appear in the response JSON.
- docs/security/read-path-redaction-matrix.md wa.state + ft robot state rows flip from `✗ LEAK` to `✓`.

### Why P1

Real leak on the default configuration — no operator opt-in required. Blast radius is narrow (titles/cwds, not pane scrollback) but automatic (fleet-wide state poll). Fix is mechanical.

 {"id":"ft-yjt9e","title":"policy::RateLimiter::pane_counts leaks entries forever — no eviction on pane destroy or GC path","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs:2076` — `RateLimiter.pane_counts: HashMap<(u64, ActionKind), Vec<Instant>>`. Every unique `(pane_id, action)` pair `check()` sees creates an entry. Nothing removes them.

A grep confirms it:

    $ rg 'pane_counts\.(remove|clear|retain)|fn gc|fn cleanup|fn prune_panes' crates/frankenterm-core/src/policy.rs
    (no matches)

## Failure shape

On a long-running ft watcher with high pane churn (the 200+-pane fleet the project is designed for), every closed pane leaves a stale `(pane_id, action)` entry in `pane_counts` — one per ActionKind the pane ever touched. Over weeks those entries accumulate:

- Each entry holds a `Vec<Instant>` of up to `limit_per_pane` entries (default 60, configurable up to u32::MAX).
- After the sliding window expires, `prune_old` empties the Vec on the next `check()` — but only if check() is called with that (pane_id, action) again, which never happens for a destroyed pane.
- The entry itself is never removed, so the HashMap grows monotonically.

For 10k destroyed panes × 5 action kinds × 16B-per-Instant × 60-entries-peak = ~48 MB of leaked state. Not a crash, but a silent drift that scales with fleet uptime.

Global side (`global_counts: HashMap<ActionKind, Vec<Instant>>`) is BOUNDED by the finite ActionKind enum — not affected.

# Fix

Add two method shapes to `impl RateLimiter`:

1. `remove_pane(&mut self, pane_id: u64)` — iterate pane_counts, retain only keys whose first element ≠ pane_id. Called from the runtime's PaneDestroyed handler (same handler that already calls `remove_runtime_pane_state_for_pane` in runtime.rs:3574).

2. `gc(&mut self)` (or `gc_at(&mut self, now: Instant)` for testability) — iterate pane_counts, for each entry call prune_old with now-window as cutoff, drop the whole entry if the resulting Vec is empty. Safe opportunistic cleanup that catches panes closed without an explicit PaneDestroyed signal (crashes, abrupt detaches).

Also add operational telemetry: `pane_entries_evicted: u64` counter so operators can see the leak being managed.

# Tests

Inline `#[cfg(test)]` in policy.rs:

- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane`
- `rate_limiter_remove_pane_leaves_other_panes_intact`
- `rate_limiter_gc_drops_expired_empty_entries`
- `rate_limiter_gc_preserves_still-active_entries`

# Severity

MEDIUM — leak is slow, bounded per pane, doesn't panic. But it's cumulative and silent. Fleet operators would only see the symptom as gradual RSS creep over uptime-weeks, attributed to anything-but-rate-limiter. Shipping the eviction path closes the hole before someone has to bisect it from production.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:51:26.703056Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.104139Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:57:11.103827Z","close_reason":"Landed as part of 27ef45e1 (absorbed commit includes ft-yjt9e methods: RateLimiter::remove_pane, gc, gc_at, tracked_pane_entry_count + 4 regression tests: remove_pane_drops_all_action_entries_for_that_pane, remove_pane_leaves_global_counts_intact, gc_drops_expired_empty_entries, gc_preserves_still_active_entries). Lib compiles clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ym8nq","title":"[review][LOW] ft-x86z2 policy gate on 5 mutating MCP tools ships without deny-path regression tests","description":"a4b7c8c3 added mcp_authorize_mcp_mutation gate at mcp_tools.rs:177-206 and wired it into 5 mutators (wa.tx_run/tx_rollback/mission_pause/resume/abort). Gate placement verified BEFORE any side effect (before mcp_resolve_mission_tx_file_path which canonicalizes fs paths; before contract/mission load; before state persist). Happy-path + allow-default tests continue to pass. But the commit explicitly defers deny-path coverage: 'Deny-policy tests per tool are scoped into a separate pass.'

Effect: the gate helper is not fenced by any test that exercises the is_denied() / requires_approval() branches. A future refactor of PolicyEngine's decision variants (or a rename of MCP_ERR_POLICY, or a change to elapsed_ms semantics in the error envelope, or a regression that breaks the early-return in the helper) would silently turn the gate into a no-op. All 5 production call sites would compile and tests would still pass, because every test path runs through an allow decision.

Scope of missing tests (5 tools x 2 deny modes = 10 missing tests, or one parameterized test per mode):
  - wa.tx_run deny -> assert envelope.ok=false, error_code=MCP_ERR_POLICY, no side effect (contract not loaded, tx not executed)
  - wa.tx_run require_approval -> assert envelope.ok=false, error_code=MCP_ERR_POLICY, hint contains 'allow-once'
  - ...same for wa.tx_rollback, wa.mission_pause/_resume/_abort

Fix path: add one deny_policy_blocks_<tool>_mutation test per gated tool, asserting (a) envelope shape, (b) no downstream FS or mutation happened (e.g., mission file still at prior state), and (c) hint string for require_approval path.

Severity LOW — not a regression today. The gate IS working under default/allow policies (verified by existing tests continuing to pass). Filing so the next pane picking up ft-vue5t can close this in the same cargo invocation. Audit round 19, 2026-04-23.
